original_hypothesis.title: MASTER_MINTER With MINTER_ROLE Gets Unexpectedly Constrained by Minter Allowance
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In mint(), the allowance check applies to any caller with MINTER_ROLE regardless of also holding MASTER_MINTER. If an address holds both roles, the MINTER_ROLE allowance check is enforced, unexpectedly limiting a MASTER_MINTER who should have unlimited minting capability. The second if-block checks hasRole(MINTER_ROLE) without excluding MASTER_MINTER holders.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a logic defect where the code contradicts the tiered minting design. MASTER_MINTER is intended to have unlimited minting authority, but holding both roles triggers allowance enforcement. This could block legitimate minting operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender())) applies allowance check without excluding MASTER_MINTER holders; should be: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender()) && !hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, _msgSender()))

preconditions: An address holds both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE simultaneously
The MINTER_ROLE allowance for that address is below the desired mint amount

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender())) { ... allowance check ... } applies regardless of MASTER_MINTER status
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: MASTER_MINTER's unlimited minting is suppressed if they also have MINTER_ROLE
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing !hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, _msgSender()) condition in the allowance check block

analysis_questions: Can addMinter be called with the MASTER_MINTER address as target?
Is there any scenario where MASTER_MINTER automatically receives MINTER_ROLE?
Does this affect the setMasterMinter flow?
validation_requirements: Test: grant both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE to same address, attempt mint exceeding allowance
Verify addMinter does not prevent adding an existing MASTER_MINTER
Check if setMasterMinter grants MINTER_ROLE alongside MASTER_MINTER

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: MASTER_MINTER address is also granted MINTER_ROLE via addMinter(masterMinterAddr, 1000). Master minter attempts mint(recipient, 5000). The access check passes (has MASTER_MINTER). But allowance check triggers (has MINTER_ROLE) and reverts because 5000 > 1000 allowance. Master minter is unexpectedly limited.
prerequisites: Same address must hold both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational disruption of minting capabilities for dual-role holders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol operations team managing token supply
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Removable by revoking MINTER_ROLE from the MASTER_MINTER address

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for dual-role (MASTER_MINTER + MINTER_ROLE) minting behavior

validation_result.mechanism: missing role exclusion in allowance check
validation_result.impact_summary: An address holding both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE has its unlimited minting authority unexpectedly constrained by the MINTER_ROLE allowance, potentially blocking legitimate minting operations.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The mint() function in ERC20ControlerMinterUpgradeable.sol has a two-part authorization logic: (1) an access check that requires the caller to have either MASTER_MINTER or MINTER_ROLE, and (2) an allowance enforcement block that triggers for any caller with MINTER_ROLE. The second block does not exclude callers who also hold MASTER_MINTER. Because the two checks are independent if-blocks (not if/else-if), a dual-role holder passes the first check via MASTER_MINTER but then gets caught by the second check via MINTER_ROLE. The design intent is clearly that MASTER_MINTER has unlimited minting — evidenced by setMasterMinter emitting MinterAllowanceUpdated with type(uint256).max and by the first if-block's logic treating MASTER_MINTER as sufficient authorization. The second if-block breaks this intent by applying the allowance limit regardless.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in the mint() function. Lines 137-138 establish the access gate (MASTER_MINTER OR MINTER_ROLE). Lines 143-147 unconditionally check and enforce the minter allowance for any MINTER_ROLE holder. No exclusion for MASTER_MINTER exists in the second block. The addMinter() function (lines ~109-113) has no guard preventing granting MINTER_ROLE to an existing MASTER_MINTER address, making the dual-role scenario achievable.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The allowance check block at 'if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender()))' applies to ALL MINTER_ROLE holders without excluding those who also hold MASTER_MINTER. A dual-role holder is limited by minterAllowed despite MASTER_MINTER being intended as unlimited. The fix is: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender()) && !hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, _msgSender()))

No guard prevents granting MINTER_ROLE to an address that already holds MASTER_MINTER, enabling the precondition for the vulnerability. The MASTER_MINTER itself (who is the role admin for MINTER_ROLE) could accidentally or intentionally call addMinter with its own address.

Emitting MinterAllowanceUpdated with type(uint256).max confirms the design intent that MASTER_MINTER should have unlimited minting capability. This intent is violated when the same address also holds MINTER_ROLE.

calling_sequence: 1. MASTER_MINTER address also gets MINTER_ROLE via addMinter(masterMinterAddr, 1000)
3. Line 137: hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, caller) returns true → first check passes (no revert)
6. Line 143: hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, caller) returns true → enters allowance block
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry point is mint(). The access check at line 137 uses logical OR to allow either MASTER_MINTER or MINTER_ROLE. A dual-role holder passes this check via MASTER_MINTER. However, the subsequent independent if-block at line 143 checks only hasRole(MINTER_ROLE) without excluding MASTER_MINTER holders. Since the caller also holds MINTER_ROLE, this block executes and enforces the minterAllowed constraint. If the mint amount exceeds the minter allowance, the transaction reverts at line 145, even though the caller should have unlimited minting authority as MASTER_MINTER.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show standard proxy deployment and owner setup. No role configuration relevant to this defect.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is straightforward and realistic: (1) MASTER_MINTER calls addMinter on its own address with some allowance, or DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE grants both roles to the same address via separate calls. (2) The dual-role holder attempts to mint an amount exceeding the MINTER_ROLE allowance. (3) The mint reverts despite the caller holding MASTER_MINTER. This is not an attack per se but a logic defect that causes operational disruption. The MASTER_MINTER could also unintentionally trigger this by calling addMinter(self, X) to set up an allowance for tracking purposes, not realizing it constrains their own unlimited minting.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM impact. The MASTER_MINTER's unlimited minting authority is unexpectedly constrained if they also hold MINTER_ROLE. This can cause operational disruption in minting operations. The workaround is straightforward: revoke MINTER_ROLE from the MASTER_MINTER address or ensure MASTER_MINTER never receives MINTER_ROLE. However, the code lacks any guard to prevent this state, and the defect contradicts the clear design intent (as shown by the type(uint256).max emission in setMasterMinter).
recommended_mitigations: Change the allowance check in mint() from 'if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender()))' to 'if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender()) && !hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, _msgSender()))' to exclude MASTER_MINTER from allowance enforcement

Alternatively, add a guard in addMinter() to prevent granting MINTER_ROLE to an address that already holds MASTER_MINTER: require(!hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, minter), 'Cannot add MASTER_MINTER as minter')
Consider restructuring mint() to use if/else-if logic: if MASTER_MINTER → unlimited, else if MINTER_ROLE → allowance check, else revert
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy EURFToken behind UUPS proxy. 2. Initialize and set owner. 3. Set a MASTER_MINTER address via setMasterMinter(). 4. From MASTER_MINTER, call addMinter(masterMinterAddress, 1000) to grant MINTER_ROLE with 1000 allowance to itself. 5. From MASTER_MINTER, call mint(someRecipient, 5000). 6. Verify the transaction reverts with mintingAllowedAmountExceeded(5000, 1000). 7. Verify that without MINTER_ROLE, the same mint(someRecipient, 5000) call succeeds.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol team intentionally wanted dual-role holders to be constrained (unlikely given the type(uint256).max emission in setMasterMinter)
Whether any operational workflow would lead to the MASTER_MINTER also receiving MINTER_ROLE in practice
additional_testing_needed: Test dual-role (MASTER_MINTER + MINTER_ROLE) minting behavior with amount exceeding allowance
Test that addMinter can be called with the MASTER_MINTER address as target
Test burn() behavior for dual-role holders (same pattern may apply if burn had allowance logic)
